Blockchain-based Systems for Securing and Sharing Forensic Evidence
===================================================================

Blockchain technology has emerged as a groundbreaking solution for securing and sharing forensic evidence in the digital age. As criminal activities become increasingly sophisticated, law enforcement agencies and forensic investigators face growing challenges in maintaining the integrity and authenticity of evidence. Blockchain-based systems offer a revolutionary approach to address these concerns by providing a decentralized, immutable, and transparent platform for managing forensic data.

The implementation of blockchain in forensics represents a paradigm shift in how evidence is collected, stored, and shared. By leveraging the inherent security features of blockchain technology, forensic professionals can establish an unalterable chain of custody for digital evidence, ensuring its admissibility in court proceedings. This innovative approach not only enhances the credibility of forensic investigations but also streamlines the process of evidence sharing among various stakeholders in the criminal justice system.

Learn about other technologies covering a wide range of Public Safety applications at the 2025 World Forum on Public Safety Technology (WF-PST) – [Sign up for Alerts.](https://engage.ieee.org/IEEE-WF-PST-Sign-Up.html)

As the field of digital forensics continues to evolve, blockchain-based systems are poised to play a crucial role in maintaining the integrity of evidence throughout its lifecycle. From crime scene documentation to laboratory analysis and courtroom presentation, blockchain technology offers a vigorous framework for securing and sharing forensic evidence. By exploring the potential applications and benefits of blockchain in forensics, law enforcement agencies and legal professionals can stay ahead of technological advancements and ensure the highest standards of evidence management in criminal investigations.

Introduction to Blockchain in Forensics
---------------------------------------

Blockchain technology is a distributed ledger system that enables secure, transparent, and tamper-resistant recording of transactions across a network of computers. Originally developed as the underlying technology for cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin, blockchain has since found applications in various industries, including forensics. At its core, [blockchain technology](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/8029379) consists of a chain of blocks, each containing a set of transactions or data entries. These blocks are cryptographically linked and distributed across multiple nodes in a network, making it extremely difficult to alter or manipulate the recorded information.

In the context of forensics, blockchain technology works by creating an immutable and transparent record of all actions taken on digital evidence. When forensic data is added to the blockchain, it is time-stamped and linked to previous entries, forming a chronological chain of events. This process ensures that every interaction with the evidence, from collection to analysis and presentation, is documented and verifiable. Smart contracts, which are self-executing programs stored on the blockchain, can be utilized to automate and enforce specific protocols in the handling of forensic evidence.

Blockchain technology is ideal for forensic evidence due to its inherent characteristics of immutability, transparency, and decentralization. The undisputable nature of blockchain ensures that once data is recorded, it cannot be altered or deleted without leaving a trace. This feature is crucial in maintaining the integrity of forensic evidence and establishing a clear chain of custody. The transparency of blockchain allows all authorized parties to view and verify the entire history of evidence handling, promoting accountability and trust in the forensic process. Additionally, the decentralized structure of blockchain eliminates single points of failure and reduces the risk of data loss or manipulation.

The benefits of using blockchain in forensics are numerous and far-reaching. Firstly, it enhances the credibility and admissibility of digital evidence in court by providing an indisputable record of its handling and analysis. This can significantly strengthen the prosecution’s case and increase the likelihood of successful convictions. Secondly, blockchain technology improves the efficiency of forensic investigations by streamlining evidence management and facilitating secure sharing of information among multiple agencies. This can lead to faster resolution of cases and better allocation of resources.

Furthermore, blockchain-based systems for forensics offer enhanced security against cyber threats and unauthorized access. The distributed nature of blockchain makes it highly resistant to hacking attempts and data breaches, protecting sensitive forensic information from malicious actors. Additionally, blockchain technology enables more effective collaboration between different law enforcement agencies and forensic experts, as it provides a standardized and interoperable platform for sharing evidence and findings.

Another significant advantage of blockchain in forensics is its potential to reduce costs associated with evidence management and storage. By eliminating the need for centralized storage systems and manual record-keeping, blockchain can lead to substantial savings in infrastructure and personnel expenses. Moreover, the use of smart contracts can automate many aspects of the forensic process, further reducing operational costs and minimizing human errors.

The introduction of blockchain technology and [smart contracts](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/321746762_Forensic-chain_Ethereum_blockchain_based_digital_forensics_chain_of_custody) in forensics represents a significant advancement in the field of digital evidence management. By leveraging the security, transparency, and efficiency of blockchain, forensic professionals can enhance the integrity of their investigations and strengthen the overall criminal justice system. As the technology continues to evolve, its potential applications in forensics are likely to expand, revolutionizing the way evidence is handled and analyzed in the digital age.

Security Enhancements through Blockchain
----------------------------------------

Blockchain technology significantly enhances the security of forensic data through its unique architectural design and cryptographic principles. At its core, blockchain secures forensic data by creating an immutable and distributed ledger of all transactions and data entries. This means that once information is recorded on the blockchain, it becomes extremely difficult to alter or delete without detection. Each block in the chain contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block, creating a linked sequence that ensures the integrity of the entire chain. If an attempt is made to tamper with any block, it would invalidate all subsequent blocks, making unauthorized modifications easily detectable.

The encryption methods used in blockchain are fundamental to its security capabilities. The most commonly used encryption technique in blockchain systems is public-key cryptography, also known as asymmetric encryption. This method employs a pair of keys: a public key for encrypting data and a private key for decryption. In the context of forensic evidence, this means that data can be securely stored and transmitted without exposing sensitive information. Additionally, blockchain platforms like the [Ethereum blockchain](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10537339/) implement advanced cryptographic algorithms such as the Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA) for secure digital signatures, ensuring the authenticity and non-repudiation of forensic data.

Preventing data tampering is one of the primary strengths of blockchain technology in forensic applications. The decentralized nature of blockchain means that multiple copies of the ledger are distributed across numerous nodes in the network. For any change to be accepted, it must be validated by a consensus of these nodes. This consensus mechanism makes it virtually impossible for a single bad actor to alter the data without being detected. Furthermore, each block in the chain contains a timestamp and a link to the previous block, creating a chronological record that cannot be easily manipulated. This feature is particularly crucial in forensics, where maintaining an unbroken chain of custody is essential for the admissibility of evidence in court.

The security protocols in blockchain are designed to protect against various types of attacks and ensure the integrity of the stored data. One of the key protocols is the use of cryptographic hash functions, which generate a fixed-size output (hash) from input data of any size. These hash functions are one-way, meaning it is computationally infeasible to derive the original input from the hash. This property is used to create digital fingerprints of forensic data, allowing for quick verification of data integrity. Another important protocol is the use of Merkle trees, which efficiently summarize all the transactions in a block, enabling quick verification of large datasets without processing the entire blockchain.

Ensuring data integrity is paramount in forensic investigations, and blockchain excels in this aspect. The combination of cryptographic hashing, consensus mechanisms, and the immutable nature of the blockchain creates a tough system for maintaining the integrity of forensic evidence. Each piece of evidence added to the blockchain is time-stamped and linked to previous entries, creating an auditable trail of all interactions with the evidence. This feature is particularly valuable in preventing and detecting any attempts at evidence tampering or data breaches.

The use of smart contracts on platforms like the Ethereum blockchain further enhances security in forensic applications. Smart contracts are self-executing programs that automatically enforce predefined rules and conditions. In forensics, smart contracts can be used to implement access control mechanisms, ensuring that only authorized personnel can interact with sensitive evidence. They can also automate the process of evidence logging and tracking, reducing the risk of human error and providing an additional layer of security.

While blockchain offers significant security enhancements, it is important to note that no system is entirely immune to threats. As with any technology, there is always the potential for vulnerabilities to be discovered and exploited. However, the decentralized nature of blockchain makes it inherently more resistant to [data breaches](https://www.weforum.org/agenda/2023/12/healthcare-data-breaches-blockchain-cybersecurity/) compared to traditional centralized systems. In the event of a security incident, the distributed ledger allows for quick identification of the affected data and facilitates rapid response and recovery.

The implementation of blockchain in forensics also addresses concerns related to long-term data preservation. Traditional digital storage methods are susceptible to degradation over time and may become obsolete as technology evolves. Blockchain’s distributed nature and the ability to store data across multiple nodes ensure that forensic evidence remains accessible and verifiable for extended periods, which is crucial for cold cases or long-running investigations.

Sharing Forensic Evidence with Blockchain
-----------------------------------------

The sharing of forensic evidence using blockchain technology represents a significant advancement in the field of [digital forensics investigation](https://www.techrxiv.org/doi/full/10.22541/au.171575721.19694510/v1). Blockchain provides a secure, transparent, and efficient platform for sharing [digital evidence](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/8626819) among various stakeholders in the criminal justice system. When forensic evidence is added to the blockchain, it is assigned a unique cryptographic identifier and time-stamped. This process creates an immutable record of when and how the evidence was shared, ensuring a clear chain of custody.

To share evidence via blockchain, authorized parties can be granted access through cryptographic keys. These keys allow them to view and verify the evidence without the ability to alter it. Smart contracts can be employed to automate the sharing process, setting predefined conditions for access and ensuring compliance with legal and procedural requirements. For example, a smart contract could be programmed to grant access to specific evidence only after obtaining necessary court orders or approvals from relevant authorities.

One of the primary advantages of blockchain for evidence sharing is its ability to maintain the integrity and authenticity of digital evidence throughout the sharing process. Traditional methods of evidence sharing often involve multiple copies and transfers, each presenting an opportunity for tampering or loss. With blockchain, the original evidence remains intact on the distributed ledger, and any attempts to modify it would be immediately detectable. This feature significantly reduces the risk of evidence contamination and enhances its admissibility in court.

Blockchain ensures evidence authenticity through its inherent properties of immutability and transparency. Each piece of digital evidence added to the blockchain is cryptographically linked to previous entries, creating a verifiable timeline of all interactions with the evidence. This chronological record allows investigators and legal professionals to trace the entire lifecycle of the evidence, from collection to analysis and presentation in court. The transparency of the blockchain also enables all authorized parties to independently verify the authenticity of the evidence, fostering trust in the forensic process.

Best practices for sharing evidence via blockchain include implementing sturdy access control mechanisms to ensure that only authorized individuals can view or interact with the evidence. This can be achieved through multi-signature protocols, where multiple parties must approve access requests. Additionally, encrypting sensitive data before adding it to the blockchain provides an extra layer of security, protecting the evidence from unauthorized access even if the blockchain itself were to be compromised.

Another important practice is the use of off-chain storage for large files or sensitive information. In this approach, only the hash of the evidence is stored on the blockchain, while the actual data is kept in secure, off-chain storage. This method balances the need for blockchain’s security features with practical considerations of data volume and privacy.

Standardization of evidence formatting and metadata is crucial for effective sharing via blockchain. Adopting common standards ensures interoperability between different agencies and systems, facilitating flawless collaboration in digital forensics investigations. This standardization also aids in the automated processing and analysis of evidence, potentially speeding up investigations and reducing the workload on forensic experts.

Implementing a comprehensive audit trail is another best practice in blockchain-based evidence sharing. This involves recording not only the evidence itself but also all access attempts, analyses performed, and conclusions drawn. Such detailed logging enhances accountability and provides valuable context for court proceedings.

It is important to note that while blockchain offers significant advantages for sharing digital evidence, it should be integrated into existing forensic workflows rather than replacing them entirely. Combining blockchain with traditional forensic tools and methodologies can create a robust ecosystem for managing and sharing digital evidence.

Challenges and Limitations
--------------------------

While blockchain technology offers significant benefits for forensic applications, it also faces several challenges and limitations that must be addressed for its widespread adoption. One of the main challenges of blockchain in forensics is the issue of scalability. As the volume of digital evidence continues to grow exponentially, blockchain networks may struggle to handle the increasing amount of data. This scalability issue stems from the fundamental design of blockchain, where each node in the network must process and store every transaction. In the context of forensics, where large files such as high-resolution images or video recordings are common, this can lead to slower transaction speeds and increased storage requirements.

Scalability affects blockchain in forensics in several ways. First, it can impact the speed at which evidence can be added to the blockchain and subsequently retrieved. In time-sensitive investigations, any delay in processing evidence could have significant consequences. Second, the increasing size of the blockchain over time may lead to storage challenges, particularly for smaller law enforcement agencies with limited resources. Potential solutions to these scalability issues include implementing off-chain storage solutions, where only the hash of large evidence files is stored on the blockchain, or exploring more efficient consensus mechanisms that can handle higher transaction volumes.

Privacy concerns represent another significant challenge in the application of blockchain to forensics. While the transparency of blockchain is generally beneficial for maintaining the integrity of evidence, it can be problematic when dealing with sensitive information. In many jurisdictions, there are strict legal requirements regarding the confidentiality of ongoing investigations and the protection of personal data. The public nature of many blockchain networks could potentially conflict with these privacy obligations. Addressing this challenge requires careful consideration of privacy-preserving techniques, such as zero-knowledge proofs or private blockchains, which allow for verification of transactions without revealing the underlying data.

The issue of regulatory compliance poses a significant challenge to the adoption of blockchain in forensics. The legal landscape surrounding digital evidence is complex and varies across jurisdictions. Implementing blockchain-based systems for forensic evidence management must comply with a wide range of regulations, including data protection laws, rules of evidence, and specific forensic standards. Ensuring that blockchain solutions meet these regulatory requirements while maintaining their core benefits of security and transparency is a delicate balance. This challenge is further complicated by the rapidly evolving nature of both blockchain technology and digital forensics, which may outpace the development of relevant legislation.

Ethical considerations also play a crucial role in the implementation of blockchain in forensics. The immutable nature of blockchain raises questions about the right to be forgotten and the potential for permanent stigmatization of individuals who are later exonerated. Additionally, the use of smart contracts in forensic processes could lead to concerns about the automation of decision-making in sensitive legal matters. Balancing the benefits of blockchain with these [ethical concerns](https://standards.ieee.org/wp-content/uploads/import/documents/other/ethical-considerations-ai-as-29mar2018.pdf) requires ongoing dialogue between technologists, legal experts, and ethicists.

The [research process](https://www.waldenu.edu/news-and-events/publications/articles/2010/01-research-challenges) in blockchain forensics presents its own set of challenges. As a relatively new field, there is a lack of standardized methodologies and tools for conducting blockchain-based forensic investigations. Developing sturdy research protocols that can withstand legal scrutiny and produce reliable, court-admissible evidence is an ongoing challenge. This includes addressing issues such as the verification of the origin of blockchain data, the interpretation of smart contract operations, and the development of forensic analysis tools specific to different blockchain platforms.

Another limitation of blockchain in forensics is the potential for conflicting versions of the truth in distributed systems. While blockchain is designed to reach consensus, in practice, there can be temporary or even permanent forks in the chain. In a forensic context, this could lead to situations where different versions of evidence exist, potentially complicating legal proceedings. Developing mechanisms to resolve such conflicts and determine the authoritative version of events is an important area of ongoing research.

The technical complexity of blockchain technology also presents a challenge in terms of training and education for forensic professionals. Many law enforcement agencies and legal professionals may lack the specialized knowledge required to effectively implement and use blockchain-based forensic systems. This knowledge gap could lead to resistance in adoption or misuse of the technology, potentially compromising investigations or legal proceedings.

Resource constraints represent another significant limitation, particularly for smaller law enforcement agencies or forensic laboratories. Implementing and maintaining a blockchain-based forensic system requires substantial computational resources and technical expertise, which may be beyond the means of many organizations. This could lead to a digital divide in forensic capabilities, where only well-funded agencies can leverage the benefits of blockchain technology.

Lastly, the issue of long-term sustainability and obsolescence must be considered. As blockchain technology continues to evolve rapidly, there is a risk that current implementations may become outdated or incompatible with future systems. Ensuring the long-term accessibility and integrity of forensic evidence stored on blockchain platforms is crucial, particularly for cold cases or appeals that may arise years or even decades after the original investigation.

Future Trends and Developments
------------------------------

The future of blockchain in forensics is poised for significant advancements and innovations that will reshape the landscape of digital evidence management. As technology continues to evolve, several trends are emerging that promise to enhance the capabilities of blockchain-based forensic systems and address current limitations.

One of the most promising future trends in blockchain for forensics is the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and [machine learning algorithms](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/8697857). This convergence of technologies has the potential to revolutionize how forensic evidence is analyzed and processed. Machine learning algorithms could be employed to automatically classify and categorize digital evidence as it is added to the blockchain, significantly reducing the workload on human investigators. AI could also be used to detect patterns or anomalies in blockchain data that might indicate fraudulent activity or tampering attempts, enhancing the security and integrity of the forensic process.

Advancements in blockchain technology are expected to have a profound impact on forensics. One area of development is the improvement of consensus mechanisms to enhance scalability and energy efficiency. For instance, the transition from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake in major blockchain networks like Ethereum is likely to address some of the scalability issues currently facing blockchain forensics. These advancements will enable faster processing of digital evidence and support the handling of larger volumes of data, crucial for keeping pace with the ever-increasing amount of digital information involved in modern investigations.

Innovations in privacy-preserving technologies are another area of expected growth in forensic blockchain applications. Zero-knowledge proofs and homomorphic encryption are techniques that allow for the verification of information without revealing the underlying data. These technologies could be instrumental in addressing the privacy concerns associated with blockchain transparency, enabling law enforcement agencies to share and verify evidence while maintaining the confidentiality of sensitive information.

The intersection of AI and blockchain in forensics is particularly exciting. Machine learning algorithms could be used to analyze blockchain data in real-time, potentially identifying criminal activities as they occur. This could lead to more proactive law enforcement strategies and faster response times to cyber threats. Additionally, AI-powered smart contracts could automate complex forensic processes, ensuring consistent application of protocols and reducing the risk of human error.

Another significant trend is the development of specialized blockchain platforms tailored specifically for forensic applications. These platforms would be designed from the ground up to meet the unique requirements of digital forensics, incorporating features such as advanced access control mechanisms, integration with existing forensic tools, and compliance with legal standards for evidence handling.

The role of blockchain in combating deep fakes and ensuring the authenticity of digital media is an area of growing importance. As the sophistication of fake audio and video content increases, blockchain could serve as a critical tool for verifying the origin and integrity of digital media evidence. By recording the hash of original media files on the blockchain at the point of creation, investigators could have a reliable method for detecting any subsequent alterations.

Interoperability between different blockchain networks is likely to become a key focus in the future of forensic applications. As various law enforcement agencies and jurisdictions adopt blockchain technology, the ability to seamlessly share and verify evidence across different platforms will be crucial. The development of cross-chain protocols and standards for evidence exchange could significantly enhance collaboration in international investigations.

The integration of Internet of Things (IoT) devices with blockchain forensics presents another frontier for innovation. As IoT devices become increasingly prevalent, they generate vast amounts of potentially valuable forensic data. Blockchain could provide a secure and tamper-evident method for logging and storing this data, creating new avenues for digital investigations.

Quantum computing poses both a threat and an opportunity for blockchain forensics. While quantum computers could potentially break current cryptographic methods, they also offer the possibility of quantum-resistant encryption algorithms that could enhance the security of blockchain systems. The development of post-quantum cryptography for blockchain is likely to be a critical area of research in the coming years.

The evolving landscape of cryptocurrency and decentralized finance (DeFi) will continue to present new challenges and opportunities for blockchain forensics. As these technologies become more sophisticated, forensic tools and techniques will need to adapt to track and analyze increasingly complex financial transactions on the blockchain.

# Potential applicability of blockchain technology in the maintenance of chain of custody in forensic casework

[*Egyptian Journal of Forensic Sciences*](/)

## Abstract

### Background

Proper investigation of digital evidence is of prime significance in cyber investigations. From the collection of evidence, its preservation, and its analysis, it is important to maintain its integrity in the legal system due to the involvement of different stakeholders like law enforcement agencies, digital analysts, and the judiciary. This review paper focuses on how blockchain technology can be used to collect evidence efficiently.

### Main text

In the present scenario, the manual logs of the chain of custody are used to ensure that the evidence remains confidential and transparent. It is accompanied by filling out the application forms and maintaining logs within the organization handling the evidence. Hence, it is important to ensure the validity, integrity, and verifiability of evidence as it moves through different hierarchical levels. There are certain issues associated with the current chain of custody, such as evidence loss, theft, tampering, and, even worse, evidence manipulation inside the system. To avoid this situation and to make the process coherent, this review paper aims to highlight the potential use of blockchain technology to preserve chain of custody.

### Conclusion

Although this scientific technology is mainly used to run cryptocurrencies, with careful consideration and application, this could play a key role in supporting and managing the chain of custody. It is a distributed database that keeps track of blocks. These blocks are collection of entries that keep growing continually and are secured from editing and manipulation by retaining the hash of the previous block in the chain. This is a decentralized technology that is not easily compromised in terms of security and therefore has the potential to solve our problem area. A future research agenda needs to be established, which lays the solid foundation for further studies on this evident emerging area.

## Background

In the legal system, evidence is utilized to convict a person or prove his or her innocence. Therefore, it is critical that the evidence’s integrity and transparency must be proven in court. Evidence collection should preserve three basic security properties: authenticity, reliability, and refutation to ensure the admissibility of the associated evidence in a court of law (Ahmad et al. [2020](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR1 "Ahmad L, Khanji S, Iqbal F et al. (2020) Blockchain-based chain of custody: towards real-time tamper-proof evidence management in ACM International Conference Proceeding Series. AssocComput Machinery. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1145/3407023.3409199
                  
                ")).

With the rapid development in cybercrime in today’s digital environment, the necessity of digital evidence for the traceability of persons linked to cybercrime is expanding. Digital evidence has its own set of issues in terms of the chain of custody (Lone and Naaz [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR15 "Lone H. and Naaz R. (2017) Forensic-chain: Ethereum blockchain based digital forensics chain of custody")).

Digital evidence, or any evidence for that matter, must pass through multiple divisions on its way from the crime scene to the courtroom. To secure the security properties, a chain of custody (CoC) is used to monitor and document the chronological history of the evidence (Giova [2011](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR11 "Giova G. (2011) Improving chain of custody in forensic investigation of electronic digital systems")). In the present scenario, the chain of custody (CoC) is maintained by keeping a logbook with records of the names of agencies that handle the evidence, which is a time-consuming and complicated method and often prone to tampering with evidence.

Blockchain is an innovative, decentralized, and distributive solution to our predicament. Blockchain technology, which promises to improve data security, privacy, and dependability, has recently piqued the curiosity of academics and policymakers. For transactions between multiple agencies, blockchain enables distributed and immutable ledgers, tamper-proof records, and built-in cryptographic capability (Valjarevic and Venter [2013](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR25 "Valjarevic A, Venter H (2013) A harmonized process model for digital forensic investigation readiness. IFIP Adv Inf Commun Technol 410(2):67–82. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-642-41148-9_5
                  
                ")).

Blockchain is a decentralized, immutable ledger that facilitates the tracking of assets and the recording of transactions within a network of an organization. In our case, assets refer to the evidence. The blockchain is an extensive chain of blocks that are linked together using cryptographic techniques that make tampering practically a challenge (Liu and Seo [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR14 "Liu Z, Seo H (2018) IoT-NUMS: evaluating NUMS elliptic curve cryptography for IoT platforms. IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 14(3):720–729. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TIFS.2018.2856123
                  
                ")).

Blockchain is usually a lengthy chain of data packets, called Blocks. Every single block is made up of several transactions. Each new block contributes to the blockchain, which is a complete log of transaction history (Billard [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR2 "Billard D. (2018) Weighted forensics evidence using blockchain. ACM International Conference Proceeding Series, Association for Computing Machinery: 57–61. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1145/3219788.3219792
                  
                ."); Prayudi [2015](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR20 "Prayudi Y (2015) Digital chain of custody: state of the art. Int J Comput Appl 114(5):1–9. 
                  https://doi.org/10.5120/19971-1856
                  
                ")). Blocks can be confirmed by the network using cryptographic techniques. Along with the transactions, each block also contains a timestamp, nonce, and hash value. The hash value of each block’s parent block and the entities link them together. This procedure contributes to the integrity of the blockchain by utilizing the Genesis block (the first block in the chain). The hash value is a one-of-a-kind integer that serves as the block’s unique identifier (Ritzdorf and Soriente [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR21 "Ritzdorf H, Soriente C et al (2018) Toward shared ownership in the cloud. IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 13(12):3019–3034. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TIFS.2018.2837648
                  
                ")).

The method of committing a crime has evolved over time. If we investigate the ratio of cybercrime to conventional crimes, the rate of cybercrime is rising exponentially across the world. The cause of this has to do with the increase in electronic gadgets and digitalization of practically everything, whether it is processing online payments through multiple portals or accessing one’s government id (Tziakouris [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR24 "Tziakouris G (2018) Cryptocurrencies - a forensic challenge or opportunity for law enforcement? An INTERPOL Perspective. IEEE Secur Priv 16(4):92–94. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/MSP.2018.3111243
                  
                ")). Block DEF is based on a blockchain-based safe and scalable digital evidence architecture (Tian et al. [2019](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR23 "Tian Z, Li M, Qiu M, Sun Y, Su S (2019) Block-DEF: a secure digital evidence framework using blockchain. Inf Sci (n y) 491:151–165. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ins.2019.04.011
                  
                ")). The solution stores the evidence on a safe platform, and the data related to the evidence is kept in the blockchain. This architecture, which combines an effective name-based byzantine fault-tolerant consensus algorithm with a mixed block format, uses a lightweight blockchain (Cebe and Erdin [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR6 "Cebe M, Erdin E et al (2018) Block4Forensic: an integrated lightweight blockchain framework for forensics applications of connected vehicles. IEEE Commun Mag 56(10):50–57. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/MCOM.2018.1800137
                  
                ")). The service layer, blockchain layer, and network layer are the three layers that makeup Block-DEF. Evidence-related services are included in the service layer, blockchain-related services and consensus protocols are included in the blockchain layer, and peer-to-peer networking is the foundation of the network layer. Block-DEF uses a multi-signature approach that uses random keys and certificated key pairs to maintain privacy and traceability (Montasari and Jahankhani [2020](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR18 "Montasari R, Jahankhani H et al (2020) Internet of things devices: digital forensic process and data reduction. Int J Electron Secur Digit Forensics 12(4):424–436. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1504/IJESDF.2020.110676
                  
                ")).

Bonomi et al. demonstrated a blockchain-based chain of custody (B-CoC) that is entirely based on a private and permission blockchain. This was decided in accordance with the CoC procedure, which prohibits unlicensed and unknown parties from managing evidence. Three parts make up B-CoC: the evidence database (a database containing digital evidence), the evidence log (which contains information on CoC and hashes on evidence), and the front-end interface (which serves as B-CoC’s user interface). The blockchain-based evidence log includes details like the evidence ID, the evidence description, the identity of the originator, and the ownership history (Li et al. [2019](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR13 "Li S, Zhao S, Yang P, Andriotis P, Xu L et al (2019) Distributed consensus algorithm for events detection in cyber-physical systems. IEEE Internet Things J 6(2):2299–2308. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/JIOT.2019.2906157
                  
                ")). The court’s chief coordinator and lightweight entities (such as forensic investigators) make up the peer-to-peer network on which the evidence log is based. The implementation process for evidence logs is broken down into three stages: the private blockchain, the private network, and finally the creation and deployment of smart contracts on top of the blockchain architecture (Lone Hamid and Mir [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR16 "Lone Hamid A, Mir RN (2017) Forensic-chain: Ethereum blockchain based digital forensics chain of custody. Sci Pract Cyber Secur J 1(2):21–27")).

Billard et al. demonstrated that digital evidence is maintained in a blockchain, which retains the evidence’s information and is available to all licensed participating parties. To enable relevant parties to assess the certainty and relevance of digital evidence, an external data structure called Forensics Confidence Rating is utilized. The Global Digital Timeline is another data structure offered, and it is used to hold the chain of events connected to digital evidence (Mezzour et al. [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR17 "Mezzour G. Frankenstein W. ley (2018) A socio-computational approach to predicting bioweapon proliferation. IEEE Trans Comput Soc Syst. 5(2):458–467. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TCSS.2018.2813529
                  
                ")).

Ahmad et al. reviewed the use of blockchain-based smart locks for physical evidence, which will be merged via smart contract with a second blockchain that will include the evidence’s metadata. The paper blockchain is built on top of the Ethereum blockchain. Ethereum is a decentralized, open-source blockchain platform that enables developers to build and deploy smart contracts and decentralized applications (DApps). It was proposed by Vitalik Buterin in late 2013 and development began in early 2014, with the network officially launching on July 30, 2015. Moreover, Ethereum introduced the concept of smart contracts, which are self-executing contracts with the terms of the agreement directly written into code (Lone Hamid and Mir [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR16 "Lone Hamid A, Mir RN (2017) Forensic-chain: Ethereum blockchain based digital forensics chain of custody. Sci Pract Cyber Secur J 1(2):21–27")). It splits the frame into 3 layers: (1) Evidence Layer, blockchain-based smart locks; (2) Blockchain Layer, blockchain-based private Ethereum fork; and (3) Network Layer, peer-to-peer communication. As compared to alternative consensus algorithm implementations, preliminary evaluation of this research results demonstrates that the proposed framework can handle realistic workloads while maintaining an acceptable transaction throughput. By documenting the chain of custody on an immutable network, blockchain protects the integrity of evidence content and its admissibility in court (Paradise [2017](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR19 "Paradise A (2017) Creation and management of social network honeypots for detecting targeted cyber attacks. IEEE Trans Comput Soc Syst 4(3):65–79. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TCSS.2017.2719705
                  
                ")).

Burri et al. proposed a method for timestamping hash values of evidence using an e-COC ledger administered by a trustworthy organization. The gathering of evidence should be carried out carefully in accordance with written SOPs or manuals. CoC ledger employs blockchain technology to prevent unauthorized changes. Moreover, certain blocks are put into a secure public blockchain to mitigate the impact of a hypothetical change to one block of the e-CoC ledger, because the public blockchain ledger is unalterable (Zou et al. [2019](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR30 "Zou D et al (2019) A Multigranularity forensics and analysis method on privacy leakage in cloud environment. IEEE Internet Things J 6(2):1484–1494. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/JIOT.2018.2838569
                  
                ")).

Prayudi et al. examined the issues encountered in the digital chain of custody as well as the efforts of scholars in giving solutions to these problems from diverse perspectives. There are still several issues that must be addressed before the digital chain of custody solutions can effectively act as an aggregation mechanism for processing evidence that will help law enforcement investigations (Zhao [2019](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR29 "Zhao D (2019) Virus propagation and patch distribution in multiplex networks: modeling, analysis, and optimal allocation. IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 14(7):1755–1767. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TIFS.2018.2885254
                  
                ")).

## Main text

### Lifecycle of digital evidence

The gathering of evidence should be carried out carefully in accordance with written standard operating procedures (SOPs) or laboratory manuals. Unfortunately, in many instances, it is not followed in forensic investigations. In terms of who first comes into correspondence with digital proof, the situation varies by country (Kumar [2021](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR12 "Gulshan Kumar (2021) Internet-of-forensic (IOF): a blockchain based digital forensics framework for IOT applications, Future Generation Computer Systems. vol:120")). There are specialized units (first reaction forces) that are taught how to operate with this type of evidence, whereas in some nations, this work is performed by law enforcement personnel who do not have qualified degrees but attained specialized training (Cosic and Cosic [2012](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR8 "Cosic J. and Cosic Z. (2012) Chain of custody and life cycle of digital evidence")). The life cycle of digital evidence is extremely complicated, and it is extremely vulnerable to tampering throughout the entire cycle, from the crime location to the courtroom. The following individuals may come into touch with the evidence:

Law enforcement officers

Forensic experts

Suspects

Victims

Eyewitness

Media personnel

The first stage in the life cycle of digital evidence is identification and collection. The exhibits are either collected by first responders or by forensic investigators, depending on the nation’s laboratory protocol (Wang and Wang [2018](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR26 "Wang S, Wang X (2018) Parallel crime scene analysis based on ACP approach. IEEE Trans Comput Soc Syst 5(1):244–255. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TCSS.2017.2782008
                  
                ")). The main goal is for an investigator to gather as much useful evidence as possible. It is a complex and difficult process to separate the relevant evidence from the irrelevant evidence. Then, the examination of evidence in forensic science laboratories. During this stage of the lifecycle, evidence encounters the forensic examiner. This is the point at which forensically significant data is extracted from vast amounts of digital data.

The third step is to prepare and present the report. This stage lists the approaches, techniques, and so on that were adopted to conduct the evidence analysis as well as the outcomes obtained after implementing them. The report is subsequently presented in court and examined by the prosecutor. Between these points, storage and transport always take place, and the chain of custody must always be maintained. After the case has been concluded or opened by the court and a verdict is issued, the evidence is maintained and archived by labs and the court, depending on their regulations (Shah and Ganesan [2019](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR22 "Shah A, Ganesan R (2019) Understanding tradeoffs between throughput, quality, and cost of alert analysis in a CSOC. IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 14(5):1155–1170. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1109/TIFS.2018.2871744
                  
                ")).

IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 14(5):1155–1170.

### Chain of custody

The chain of custody is a fundamental and critical process in the legal system, particularly in criminal and civil law cases. It refers to the chronological documentation and tracking of physical evidence from the moment it is collected at a crime scene or discovered through an investigation, through its handling, storage, and presentation in court. Typically, the chain of custody involves detailed records, including the names of individuals who had custody of the evidence, the dates and times of transfers, descriptions of the evidence, and any relevant information about its storage conditions (D’Anna [2023](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR9 "D’Anna T (2023) The chain of custody in the modern era of modern forensics: from the classic procedures for gathering evidence to the new challenges related to digital data. Healthcare 11(5):634")). The fact of the matter is that after evidence is gathered, the transfer of the evidence among all entities must be documented for the evidence to be acknowledged. Additionally, it must be demonstrated that the chain of custody is reliable. Nobody can acquire evidence unless the chain of custody is documented. And no one can alter the chain of custody record. If we can show that there are discrepancies in the chain of custody, the court will throw out all the evidence (Yan and Shen [2020](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR27 "Yan W., Shen J (2020) Blockchain based digital evidence chain of custody. ACM International Conference Proceeding Series, Association for Computing Machinery, pp. 19–23. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1145/3390566.3391690
                  
                ")).

In a court of law, physical and digital evidence are used in tandem to establish whether something is true or untrue. As a result, we might conclude that managing both physical and digital evidence is critical. Figure [1](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#Fig1) depicts both physical and digital evidence that will aid in forensic activities.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs41935-023-00383-w/MediaObjects/41935_2023_383_Fig1_HTML.png)

Structure of blockchain

The present system of maintaining the chain of custody consists of the accompanying agency filling out forms and putting them in a forensic lab or forwarding them to the prosecutor as well as keeping a logbook with all the documentation. The issue arises here because, in many circumstances, the chain of custody is taken for granted by a few policemen, scientific personnel, or any other authorized entity through which evidence transits throughout its life cycle. To strengthen the case in court, the conditions outlined in the chain of custody must be met.

*Trustworthy*: The chain of custody should be sufficiently reliable for the court that it cannot be called into question. The agency involved in the process should ensure that the proper SOPs and procedures are followed throughout the process’s life cycle.

*Auditable*: At any point in time, the chain of custody should be auditable. It should be kept as transparent as possible. Every entity involved in the process must be able to verify the entire process.

*Traceable*: The evidence must be tracked from its collection to its examination.

*Tamper-proof*: The evidence should be kept secure and without any hindrance.

*Integrity*: The evidence’s integrity and confidentiality should be preserved, which means it should not have been altered or corrupted during the transfer.

### Challenges to chain of custody

Blockchain technology has shown to have enormous potential for reducing scalability- and transparency-required traditional validation procedures. There are certain challenges to chain of custody which are as follows:

*Excessive paperwork*: In the current scenario in India, an exhibit transfer between different agencies involves far too much paperwork, which includes the name of the agency accompanying the evidence, case number, transport permit, condition of evidence, name of person with evidence, time, integrity of stamp, and so on, consuming a significant amount of time. Acceptance of evidence for examination in a forensic science lab (FSL) might take up to a day.

*Tampering/contamination potential*: The possibility of tampering with evidence is always significant, and numerous incidents have been documented in which either the evidence was contaminated or interfered with during the transfer of evidence from one agency to another and the record was clear in the chain of custody. The issue could be understood with the help of Fig. [2](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#Fig2) by considering the case in the figure, where digital evidence was retrieved from the crime scene and was meant to be sent to the FSL, but the data on a storage device was tampered with by the first responder and then submitted to the FSL for assessment.

*Justifying the chain’s integrity in court*: The requirement that a piece of evidence be proven to be genuine, that is what its presenter claims it to be, is referred to as authentication or identification of true evidence. To achieve this, the chain of custody’s integrity must be proven in a high court of law (Giannelli [1996](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR10 "Giannelli P. C (1996) Chain of custody. Law Scholarly Commons School of Law Scholarly Commons Faculty Publications")). This is the most critical challenge to the current system for maintaining chain of custody, as any minor flaw by the first responder or other agencies is enough for the defense to hinder the court, resulting in the case being dismissed and the suspect walking free from court even if he or she is guilty.

*Tracking interaction with exhibits*: Tracking down who had contact with the evidence when it was transferred to labs from a police station, or a crime scene is difficult to trust since the individual indicated on record may become antagonistic and tamper with the evidence.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs41935-023-00383-w/MediaObjects/41935_2023_383_Fig2_HTML.png)

### Blockchain in the maintenance of chain of custody

In this section, we will cover our approach based on previous studies done in this domain, Information about the “BASE-64 Algorithm,” as well as the blockchain as a service in the chain of custody and the advantages it offers.

Blockchain as stakeholder: As previously stated, blockchain is a distributed ledger system that is highly encrypted, can be inspected at any point, and can avoid unauthorized access while retaining data integrity and confidentiality (Chopade and Khan [2019](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR7 "Chopade M., Khan S (2019) Third International conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC). IEEE")). Blockchains are classified into three types: public, private, and consortium blockchains. A public blockchain network is one in which anybody who is connected to the network may freely read and publish by using a private blockchain organizations may control who gets access to blockchain data, Certain data sets can only be viewed by those who have been granted authority (Zhang et al. [2021](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR28 "Zhang J, Hong Zhong, Chengjir Gu, Lu Liu (2021) Secure and efficient certificateless provable data possession for cloud-based data management systems. Lecture Notes in Computer Science (including subseries Lecture Notes in Artificial Intelligence and Lecture Notes in Bioinformatics), vol. 12681 LNCS. p. 71–87. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-73194-6_5
                  
                ")). The consensus process in a consortium blockchain network is carefully governed by a set number of nodes or stakeholders.

Base64 encryption: Base64 is an encoding system for binary data that transforms it into ASCII text format. It organizes the data and expresses it exactly in 6 bits. The use of Base64 restricted. It uses an algorithm to translate binary data and can incorporate pictures, audio, and video assets in text format (Chopade and Khan [2019](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR7 "Chopade M., Khan S (2019) Third International conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC). IEEE")). The encoded textual data provides simple transmission over networks with no data loss. The first 62 values are from A to Z, a-z, and 0 to 9. The symbols + and / are also used. After processing with the Base64 technique, the combination of these 64 characters is utilized to build a hash value of the data as output.

Framework of the blockchain-based CoC: In the framework, the first responder’s major responsibility after collecting evidence from the crime scene is to protect the evidence, then capture volatile data and record down every detail such as the type of evidence, size, hash value, and so on. The digital forensic expert must clone the data of digital evidence because it is a fundamental principle of forensic science to never operate on the original digital exhibit. After the cloning process is completed, the case’s IO will upload the cloned data to the blockchain, and the physical exhibit will be packed, sealed, photographed, and stored in the blockchain-based smart lock vault in the police station until the evidence is produced in court. The data can then be retrieved by authorized staff of the concerned entity who have been assigned to work on the evidence by inputting the case id and their private key on the chain (Bradford and Ray [2007](/articles/10.1186/s41935-023-00383-w#ref-CR4 "Bradford PG, Ray DA (2007) Using digital chains of custody on constrained devices to verify evidence. IEEE International Conference on Intelligence and Security Informatics, ISI 2007, New Brunswick, New Jersey, pp 23–24")).

Abstract
--------

Blockchain technology has gained significant attention in recent years for its potential to revolutionize various sectors, including finance, supply chain management, and digital forensics. While blockchain’s decentralization enhances security, it complicates the identification and tracking of illegal activities, making it challenging to link blockchain addresses to real-world identities. Also, although immutability protects against tampering, it introduces challenges for forensic investigations as it prevents the modification or deletion of evidence, even if it is fraudulent. Hence, this paper provides a systematic literature review and examination of state-of-the-art studies in blockchain forensics to offer a comprehensive understanding of the topic. This paper provides a comprehensive investigation of the fundamental principles of blockchain forensics, exploring various techniques and applications for conducting digital forensic investigations in blockchain. Based on the selected search strategy, 46 articles (out of 672) were chosen for closer examination. The contributions of these articles were discussed and summarized, highlighting their strengths and limitations. This paper examines the selected papers to identify diverse digital forensic frameworks and methodologies used in blockchain forensics, as well as how blockchain-based forensic solutions have enhanced forensic investigations. In addition, this paper discusses the common applications of blockchain-based forensic frameworks and examines the associated legal and regulatory challenges encountered in conducting a forensic investigation within blockchain systems. Open issues and future research directions of blockchain forensics were also discussed. This paper provides significant value for researchers, digital forensic practitioners, and investigators by providing a comprehensive and up-to-date review of existing research and identifying key challenges and opportunities related to blockchain forensics.

1. Introduction
---------------

Blockchain has emerged as one of the most revolutionary advancements of the digital age, transforming a multitude of domains with its unique attributes of decentralization, immutability, and transparency. It was originally regarded as the underlying technology for Bitcoin; however, blockchain has rapidly expanded beyond cryptocurrencies to impact industries such as finance, supply chain management, healthcare, and many others [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B1-electronics-13-03568)]. Its ability to create secure and tamper-proof ledgers ensures data integrity and trust without the need for intermediaries, which reduces costs and increases efficiency. As a foundational technology, blockchain’s influence is expanding, promising to redefine trust, security, and efficiency in the digital age [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B2-electronics-13-03568)].

While blockchain offers numerous advantages across various domains, it brings security and forensic challenges. The anonymous nature of blockchain transactions complicates the identification of individuals involved in illegal activities. The decentralized and immutable characteristics of blockchain also make it difficult to alter or remove fraudulent or malicious transactions once they are recorded, posing a unique set of challenges for investigators attempting to trace and rectify such actions [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B3-electronics-13-03568)]. Furthermore, the global and borderless nature of blockchain networks means that illegal activities can span multiple jurisdictions, complicating legal processes and international cooperation. The complexity of blockchain protocols and the rapid development of new blockchain-based technologies, such as privacy coins and Decentralized Finance (DeFi) platforms, introduce additional layers of difficulty in monitoring and investigating suspicious activities [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B4-electronics-13-03568)].

Traditional digital forensic frameworks are inadequate for handling blockchain investigations due to the distinct challenges posed by blockchain. Unlike conventional systems where data can be centrally accessed and manipulated, blockchain’s decentralized nature means that data are distributed across a vast network of nodes, making it difficult to isolate and analyse. The anonymity of blockchain transactions further complicates the identification of individuals involved in illegal activities, as addresses and transactions do not inherently link to real-world identities [[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B5-electronics-13-03568)]. Also, the immutable characteristic of blockchain poses a challenge for forensic investigators adapted to the possibility of recovering deleted files or analysing logs for traces of manipulation. Additionally, the rapid evolution of blockchain protocols and the emergence of complex features like smart contracts and privacy coins add another layer of complexity, requiring specialized tools and expertise that traditional frameworks lack [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B6-electronics-13-03568)].

Blockchain forensics is a critical field that addresses the unique challenges posed by blockchain in digital forensic investigations. The decentralized, immutable, and anonymous nature of blockchain transactions makes traditional forensic techniques inadequate for tracing illegitimate activities and securing digital evidence. To combat these challenges, effective frameworks that utilize blockchain-based forensic solutions should be used. These solutions offer significant benefits in the digital forensic investigation of various domains by leveraging the inherent strengths of blockchain technology, such as transparency, immutability, and decentralization. These solutions enhance the integrity and reliability of digital evidence by ensuring that once data are recorded on the blockchain, it cannot be altered or deleted, thereby preserving a tamper-proof chain of custody. This immutability is crucial for maintaining the credibility of evidence in legal proceedings. Additionally, the transparent nature of blockchain allows for real-time verification and auditing of transactions, making it easier to trace and link illegal activities across different platforms [[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B7-electronics-13-03568),[8](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B8-electronics-13-03568)].

This paper aims to provide a comprehensive systematic literature review that follows the PRISMA 2020 protocol designed by Page et al. [[9](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/13/17/3568#B9-electronics-13-03568)], which investigates and evaluates state-of-the-art studies in blockchain forensics, a crucial field in digital forensics that addresses the unique challenges posed by blockchain. The primary objective of this paper is to systematically identify, evaluate, and summarize the current state of research in blockchain forensics. This involves a thorough review of the literature to capture recent advancements, methodologies, and frameworks used in the field. By synthesizing findings from various studies, this paper aims to provide a comprehensive overview of how blockchain forensics has evolved and the contributions of different research efforts. Another key objective is to assess the various digital forensic frameworks and methodologies employed specifically in blockchain contexts, as well as evaluate the impact of blockchain-based forensic solutions on digital forensic investigations. Based on the selected search strategy, 46 articles (out of 672) were chosen for closer examination. The contributions of these articles were discussed and summarized, highlighting their strengths and limitations. This paper conducts a comprehensive review of selected literature to identify various digital forensic frameworks and methodologies employed in blockchain forensics. It also assesses how blockchain-based forensic solutions have contributed to the advancement of digital forensic investigations. Furthermore, this paper highlights the common applications of blockchain-based forensic frameworks and explores the legal and regulatory challenges encountered in conducting forensic investigations within blockchain systems. Open issues and future research directions of blockchain forensics are also discussed.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Blockchain enhances evidence tracking by creating an immutable, transparent ledger that secures the chain of custody, ensuring integrity and authenticity of digital evidence in forensic investigations.

enriched_points.expanded_content: AI and machine learning are revolutionizing forensic medicine by automating evidence analysis, identifying patterns in data, and improving accuracy in detecting anomalies during investigations.
